title: The Person and Work of Christ, Part 7: The Estate of Humiliation
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: 5-26-24 - Person and Work of Christ - Part 7 - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Christ's Emptying of Himself (Kenosis)
subpoints: What does 'emptied himself' mean in Philippians 2:7?
The Kenosist theory — and why it is rejected
Chalcedonian definition: two natures, no confusion or separation
Christ retained all divine attributes even in humiliation

outline.title: WLC Q47: Humiliation in Conception and Birth
subpoints: Born in the fullness of time (Galatians 4:4-5)
Born of a woman of lowest estate
Swaddling cloth and a manger — more than ordinary abasement
The promise of Genesis 3:15 fulfilled

outline.title: WLC Q48: Humiliation in His Life
subpoints: Subjected to the law — active and passive obedience begin
Fulfilling what Adam failed to do: the covenant of works
Conflict with the indignities of the world — despised and rejected
Temptation by Satan in the wilderness
Infirmities of the flesh: hunger, exhaustion, sorrow

outline.title: WLC Q49: Humiliation in His Death
subpoints: Betrayed by Judas, forsaken by disciples, condemned by Pilate
Bore the wrath and curse of God in our place
Crucified — the cursed death of the cross
Deuteronomy 27 curses fulfilled in Christ

outline.title: WLC Q50: Humiliation After His Death
subpoints: 'Descended into hell' = burial and remaining under power of death
Three days and three nights in the heart of the earth
Christ bore the wages of sin even in burial
His burial is part of the gospel (1 Corinthians 15:3-4)

summary: In this seventh lesson of the Person and Work of Christ Sunday School series, Mr. Isaiah Couch walks through Westminster Larger Catechism questions 46–50 on Christ's estate of humiliation. He begins by addressing the Kenosist theory, arguing from Philippians 2:5-7 and the Chalcedonian definition that Christ emptied himself not of his divine attributes but of his glory, taking on the form of a servant while remaining truly and fully God. He then traces the four stages of humiliation identified by the catechism: his conception and birth in lowly circumstances, his life under the law facing indignities, temptation, and infirmities, his death by betrayal, condemnation, and the bearing of God's wrath on the cross, and finally his burial and remaining under the power of death for three days. Throughout, Couch stresses that these realities cannot be rightly understood apart from the Chalcedonian two-natures Christology — the mediator is the true God-man, and every act of humiliation was accomplished by him without any diminishment of his divine nature.
transcript:
## Opening Prayer and Introduction

Good morning, everyone. Let us go to our garden prayer before we begin. Almighty Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. We thank you for the blessing of being able to gather with your people on your Lord's Day to in this time of learning more about the person and work of Christ. I pray that during our time I would be made nothing that you would call the nerves within me and that you would be made everything as we look to our Savior and the work that he accomplished in the incarnation and his life and his death and then being buried until the resurrection. We thank you, pray that you bless our time in your name. Amen.

Well, for those who are visiting, my name is Isaiah Couch. I am a member here at our old church. I am a member here and it is a joy the session asked me every now and then to speak during Sunday school hour and so it is a joy to be able to do that this morning. In regards to our lesson material, the past three weeks we have been focusing on examining a question in our larger catechism. The last three weeks have really been focusing on unpacking question 42 in our larger catechism. The past three weeks we have examined Christ in his threefold office as prophet, priest and king and this morning we come to his estate of humiliation. Next week, Lord willing, I will be able to speak on the estate of exaltation.

## Christ's Emptying of Himself — The Kenosis Question

But this morning I want to focus on this one area: Christ's humiliation and his work. Question 46 asks, what was the estate of Christ's humiliation? The estate of Christ's humiliation was that low condition wherein he for our sakes, emptying himself of his glory, took upon him the form of a servant, in his conception and birth and life and death and after his death until his resurrection.

When speaking of Christ's estate of humiliation our catechism speaks here of it being primarily in four parts: his incarnation, his life, his death and after his death until his resurrection. Some have presented a fifth element — the emptying himself of his glory — which comes before and within the incarnation. We read in Philippians 2:5-7 that our Lord Jesus Christ who being in the form of God did not consider it robbery to be equal with God but made himself of no reputation taking the form of a bond servant and coming in the likeness of men.

The question often arises: what exactly did Christ empty himself of? Our catechism says he emptied himself of his glory. Are we to understand that Christ emptied himself of his divinity or some of his divine attributes? The Westminster Assembly understood that the expression 'emptied himself' cannot mean that the Son of God ceased to be God — truly and fully God — which also means that there is no way he could have emptied himself of his divine attributes.

There is a belief in some Lutheran and evangelical circles often called the Kenosist theory. It basically believes that Christ in some way emptied himself of his divine attributes, mainly his omnipotence and omniscience. The Greek verb translated 'emptied' does not mean that the Son emptied himself by losing or setting aside or refraining from using any part of his divine nature. The manner in which he emptied himself is explained in the reality of him taking something to himself — taking the form of a bond servant and coming in the likeness of men.

In Chalcedon, 451, with regards to the person of Christ: the two natures undergo no confusion, no change, no division, no separation. At no point was the difference between the natures taken away through the union, but rather the property of both natures is preserved. It comes together into a single person and a single subsistent being. Christ himself also declares that even in his estate of humiliation, he truly and fully possessed the divine nature and all divine attributes. He says in John 10 that no one takes his life from him, but he lays it down of himself. He exercised omnipotence and omniscience in the providential work of upholding the universe (Colossians 1:15-17; Hebrews 1:3). As Warfield rightly said, we cannot afford to lose either the God in the man or the man in the God.

## Humiliation in His Conception and Birth (WLC Q47)

Question 47 asks, how did Christ humble himself in his conception and birth? Christ humbled himself in his conception and birth in that being from all eternity the Son of God in the bosom of the Father, he was pleased in the fullness of time to become the Son of man, made of a woman of lowest state and to be born of her with diverse circumstances of more than ordinary abasement.

This echoes Galatians 4:4-5: when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law that we might receive the adoption of sons. The fullness of time refers to the appointed time decreed from all eternity and also the first promise in Genesis 3:15 — the seed of the woman who is Christ. And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14).

Our Lord Jesus Christ was not born into earthly wealth and glory. He was born in a lowest state, in an animal stable, clothed in swaddling cloth, placed into a feeding trough. Luke 2:7: she brought forth her firstborn son and wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no room for them in the inn. This is more than ordinary abasement. Even the appearance of the heavenly hosts before lowly shepherds is a form of humiliation — the one who could command every knee to bow lay silent in the feeding trough.

## Humiliation in His Life (WLC Q48)

Question 48 asks, how did Christ humble himself in his life? Christ humbled himself in his life by subjecting himself to the law, which he perfectly fulfilled, and by conflicting with the indignities of the world, temptations of Satan, and infirmities in his flesh.

In Matthew 5 Jesus says he came not to destroy the law but to fulfill it. It was necessary for the mediator of the covenant to fulfill what the first Adam by his sin failed to accomplish — the covenant of works. Christ is the true and better Adam. Romans 5: as by one man's disobedience many were made sinners, so also by one man's obedience many will be made righteous.

Isaiah 53:3 prophesies that Christ would be despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief. He came to his own and his own did not receive him. He also faced the temptations of Satan (Matthew 4), and the infirmities in his flesh — hunger, thirst, exhaustion, and sorrow. Hebrews 2:17-18: in all things he had to be made like his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest.

As J. G. Voss says, we should be filled with the deepest gratitude to him who endured such affliction and provision for our sake, and we should resist the temptation to yield to discouragement and despair when we face troubles and hardships, remembering that our Savior endured much more grievous troubles because of his great love for us.

## Humiliation in His Death (WLC Q49)

Question 49 asks, how did Christ humble himself in his death? Christ humbled himself in his death in that having been betrayed by Judas, forsaken by his disciples, scorned and rejected by the world, condemned by Pilate and tormented by his persecutors, having also conflicted with the terrors of death and the powers of darkness, felt and borne the weight of God's wrath, he laid down his life an offering for sin, enduring the painful, shameful and cursed death of the cross.

The betrayal of Judas, the forsaking by his disciples, and the unjust condemnation by Pilate fulfilled Isaiah 53. It was in this same passage that we see the humiliation of Christ's death — being stricken, smitten by God, wounded for our transgressions, bruised for our iniquities, led as a lamb to the slaughter. Deuteronomy 27 pronounces curses on those who break the covenant, and Christ took our place as the covenant-breaker. Galatians 3:13: Christ has redeemed us from the curse of the law, having become a curse for us, for it is written, cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree.

Acts 2: being delivered by the determined purpose and foreknowledge of God, you have taken by lawless hands, have crucified and put to death. It was our sins that put to death the Lord Jesus Christ — it was the serpent who wounded him out of hatred, but it was all by the decree of God from before the foundation of the world.

## Humiliation After His Death (WLC Q50)

Our last question this morning: wherein consisted Christ's humiliation after his death? Christ's humiliation after his death consisted in his being buried, and continuing in the state of the dead, and under the power of death till the third day — otherwise expressed in the words 'he descended into hell.'

Our catechism communicates that 'descended into hell' does not refer to Christ going to the place of torment to preach to the spirits. The words refer to Christ's state of humiliation in being buried and continuing under the power of death for a time. Jesus himself says in Matthew 12 that as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.

Paul declares in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 that this is necessary for the gospel: Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose again the third day. His death and burial are historic realities, not merely spiritual. Isaiah 53: they made his grave with the wicked, but with the rich at his death. Christ being buried and remaining in the state of the dead, he bore the wages of our sin (Romans 6:23). Hebrews 2:14: through death he might destroy him who had the power of death, that is the devil, and release those who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage.

## Closing Thoughts and Discussion

While it is difficult to think about Christ's estate of humiliation without looking forward to his estate of exaltation, it is important that we reflect on that estate. It was the God-man who was clothed in flesh, born of a woman of low estate, lived a life of more than ordinary abasement, suffered a humiliating death on the cross by becoming a curse for the sins of his people, and descended to the lowest part of his humiliation and remaining for a time under the power of death in the tomb. This is the humiliation of Christ. This is our Lord, the mediator of the covenant of grace.

As Philippians 2:5-7 says, who being in the form of God did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bond servant, and coming in the likeness of men.

Let us pray. Our Almighty God, we thank you for the work of our mediator in his humiliation. We thank you that in the beginning he was God and he is God. And we thank you that he entered into time, that he condescended, that he humiliated himself by taking on flesh, to be born under the law, to be obedient to your law, to redeem your people, and that he bore the wrath that we deserve, and that he was buried, that he took on the terrors of death for his people. We cannot fully comprehend the depth of his humiliation, but we thank you for it, because it is the only way that we can know you. We thank you that it does not cease in his humiliation, but we look forward to next week when we speak about his exaltation, and him seated at your right hand, reigning forevermore. Amen.
